title: Antarctica's Blood Falls: Why a Glacier Bleeds Red
description: Taylor Glacier in Antarctica oozes red water from a crack in the ice — and the cause was locked away for over a million years. The iron-rich brine comes from an ancient subglacial lake sealed in total darkness, where iron slowly leached from bedrock with no oxygen. A 2017 study by Jill Mikucki confirmed the source using geochemical tracing. #Antarctica #BloodFalls #Geography #Science #TaylorGlacier

segments.voiceover: A glacier in Antarctica bleeds red — and the water pouring out has been trapped underground for over a million years.
segments.broll_cue: close-up of Blood Falls red water flowing from Taylor Glacier ice face

segments.voiceover: It's called Blood Falls, on Taylor Glacier in the McMurdo Dry Valleys. Beneath it sits a saltwater lake, sealed under ice for around 1.5 million years. In 2017, geochemist Jill Mikucki confirmed the red color comes from iron in that brine — iron that slowly dissolved from bedrock in a completely oxygen-free environment.
segments.broll_cue: aerial view of Taylor Glacier and McMurdo Dry Valleys, Antarctica

segments.voiceover: The moment that iron-rich water hits open air, it rusts. Instantly. So the glacier isn't bleeding — it's oxidizing in real time. And that sealed lake underneath? It still contains living microbes, surviving with no light, no oxygen, and almost no heat.
segments.broll_cue: close-up of rust-red brine seeping across white glacier ice at Blood Falls

segments.voiceover: Follow for one real fact a day.
segments.broll_cue: wide shot of Blood Falls against the stark white Taylor Glacier

fact_check_flags: 'over a million years' — source material states 1.5 million years; verify this figure against Mikucki et al. 2017 (Nature Geoscience) and confirm it refers to isolation duration, not lake age
Jill Mikucki as lead author/geochemist on the 2017 study — confirm name spelling, title, and institutional affiliation
2017 as the year the iron-source mechanism was confirmed — verify publication date of the Nature Geoscience paper
'oxygen-free environment' — confirm the subglacial lake is described as anoxic in the source paper
'living microbes' in the subglacial brine — confirm this is documented in peer-reviewed literature and not contested
Script says iron 'rusts instantly' on air contact — verify whether oxidation is visually rapid or gradual; 'instantly' may be an overstatement
Taylor Glacier location in McMurdo Dry Valleys — confirm this is geographically accurate
Hook says 'over a million years' while SPECIFIC says '1.5 million years' — reconcile for consistency before posting

source_idea.title: Antarctica's Blood Falls
source_idea.hook: In Antarctica, a glacier bleeds red. Scientists discovered why only in 2017 — and the answer was hiding for 100 million years.
source_idea.fact_summary: Blood Falls (Taylor Glacier, Antarctica) flows red because of iron oxide in saltwater from an ancient subglacial lake sealed beneath the ice for 1.5 million years. In 2017, researchers using geochemistry proved the iron came from chemical weathering of bedrock in an oxygen-free environment, creating ferrous iron that turns red when exposed to air.
source_idea.source_hint: Wikipedia: Blood Falls or Nature Geoscience (Mikucki et al., 2017 study on Taylor Glacier)
